addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Gauntlet exit. A failure here is a real migration bug (a missed site), not
  exploration — triage is opus-grade.
  
  ## Goal
  
  The whole sprue migration proven end-to-end, and the discoverability promise
  verified: one grep per family returns the complete cluster, no bare values
  survive.
  
  ## Recipe
  
  - Run the gauntlet (`tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`) — pre-flight `rbw-MZ`,
    all crucibles quenched, per burn-bridges posture.
  - Grep audit:
    - `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/` / `rbnne_` / `bunne_` each return a complete,
      coherent cluster.
    - `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
    - Spot bare-value audit: surviving `conjure`/`graft`/`tether`/`airgap`/
      `disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` hits are all class-1/3/4
      (prose, identifiers, coupled derived strings), zero class-2.
  
  ## Acceptance
  
  - Gauntlet green.
  - Grep audit clean per above.

content.content:
1	## Context
2	
3	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
4	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
5	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
6	`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
7	across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
8	fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
9	enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
10	work, prone to silent drift.
11	
12	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
13	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
14	uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
15	the sweep.
16	
17	The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
18	messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
19	durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
20	discovery recipe, not here.
21	
22	## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay
23	
24	"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
25	literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
26	character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
27	API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
28	textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
29	`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
30	build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.
31	
32	MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
33	an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
34	the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
35	prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
36	(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
37	inventing rationale.
38	
39	Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
40	dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
41	local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
42	(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
43	one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
44	text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
45	concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.
46	
47	## Convention — locked
48	
49	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
50	
51	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
52	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
53	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
54	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
55	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
56	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
57	             `e` = environment, etc.)
58	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
59	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
60	
61	Full target table (all in-scope enums):
62	
63	| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
64	|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
65	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
66	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
67	| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
68	| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
69	| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
70	| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |
71	
72	Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
73	egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
74	share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
75	DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
76	variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
77	whole cluster.
78	
79	## Scope decisions — resolved
80	
81	- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
82	- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
83	  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
84	  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
85	- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
86	  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
87	  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
88	- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
89	  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
90	  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
91	  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
92	  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
93	  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
94	  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
95	  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
96	- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
97	  unchanged from original framing).
98	
99	## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work
100	
101	Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
102	the candidate list and classify each hit:
103	
104	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
105	   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
106	2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
107	   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
108	   **Migrate.**
109	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
110	   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
111	   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
112	   alone.**
113	4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
114	   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
115	   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
116	   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
117	   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
118	     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
119	   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
120	   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
121	   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
122	   `-tether`.
123	
124	### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses
125	
126	- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
127	  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
128	  `:-bind`, etc.
129	- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
130	  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
131	  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
132	  stale.
133	- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
134	  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
135	  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
136	  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
137	  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).
138	
139	### Noise ranking (classification effort)
140	
141	Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
142	prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
143	`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
144	class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
145	is the heavy classification grind.
146	
147	## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate
148	
149	Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
150	(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
151	context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
152	literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
153	declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
154	remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
155	until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.
156	
157	**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
158	sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
159	graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
160	suites.
161	
162	## Discovery recipe
163	
164	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
165	  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
166	  enum missed.
167	- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
168	  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
169	- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
170	  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
171	  tables.
172	- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
173	  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
174	  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.
175	
176	## Posture
177	
178	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
179	execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
180	pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.
181	
182	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
183	`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
184	gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
185	remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
186	above.
187	
188	## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)
189	
190	₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
191	(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
192	not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
193	concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
194	wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
195	is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
196	intact.
197	
198	What remains and matters:
199	- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
200	  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
201	  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
202	  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
203	- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
204	  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
205	- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
206	  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
207	  any late bugfix churn there.
208	
209	## What done looks like
210	
211	- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
212	  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
213	- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
214	  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
215	  profiles) updated in place.
216	- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
217	  their selecting dispatch migrated.
218	- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
219	- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
220	- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
221	  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
222	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.
223	
224	## Out of scope
225	
226	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
227	  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
228	- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
229	  by decision above.
230	- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
231	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.
232	
233	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
234	
235	Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:
236	
237	1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
238	   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
239	   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
240	   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
241	2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
242	   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
243	3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
244	   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
245	   graft cloud build.
246	4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
247	   heavy classification.
248	5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
249	6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.
250	
251	Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
252	not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; node-regime modes (`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/
`allowlist`) for the `RBRN_*` family. These bare values appear scattered
across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps), and Rust (test
fixtures), with no cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its
enum family. This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace
work, prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works but
uses a different shape from what this heat adopts; harmonization is part of
the sweep.

The inventory pace has been completed (see heat log / slate commit
messages for depth). This paddock records the resolved scope and the
durable findings; line-numbered site lists deliberately live in the
discovery recipe, not here.

## MCM grounding — "sprue" is `mcm_sprue`, prefix discipline makes it an inlay

"Sprue" here is not a coined word — it is the MCM quoin `mcm_sprue`: *a
literal wire-level token representing a serialized name — the exact
character sequence as it appears in a wire format (a JSON property key, an
API field name, a protocol identifier).* Our regime enum values are
textbook sprues: `rbnve_conjure` is the verbatim token that appears in
`rbrv.env`, in the `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` Cloud Build substitution, in
build-info JSON, and in Python/shell comparisons.

MCM also states: *a sprue that follows the prefix naming discipline is also
an `mcm_inlay`* (prefix-recognized, not lexicon-catalogued). So precisely,
the values are *already* sprues even while bare; what this heat adds is the
prefix discipline that makes each sprue **also an inlay**. The spec pace
(₢BMAAA) should cite `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding rather than
inventing rationale.

Note on a sibling usage: ₣BK independently uses "sprue" for tabtarget
dispatch tokens (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}`, e.g. `rbml_rbw`) and wrote a
local gloss in BCG/CLAUDE.md. That is *also* a valid `mcm_sprue` instance
(a literal dispatch-wire token) — not a competing definition, a sibling
one. Both heats correctly name wire-level literal tokens "sprue." ₣BK's BCG
text would ideally cite `mcm_sprue` rather than read as a freshly-minted
concept, but that is ₣BK's documentation concern, not a blocker here.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Full target table (all in-scope enums):

| Variable                 | Regime | Bare → Sprue                                          |
|--------------------------|:------:|-------------------------------------------------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE         | v      | bind/conjure/graft → `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE         | v      | tether/airgap → `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`              |
| RBRN_ENTRY_MODE          | n      | disabled/enabled → `rbnne_disabled`/`_enabled`        |
| RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE     | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE  | n      | disabled/global/allowlist → `rbnne_*`                 |
| BURN_PLATFORM            | n      | bubep_linux/mac/windows → `bunne_linux`/`_mac`/`_windows` |

Family-sharing is a deliberate property, not an accident: vessel-mode and
egress-mode share `rbnve_` (both vessel-regime); the three node-mode enums
share `rbnne_`, so `rbnne_disabled` is intentionally voiced by ENTRY,
DNS, and ACCESS alike. Uniqueness lives in the full quoin + the owning
variable, not in the 5-char prefix. A single grep per family returns the
whole cluster.

## Scope decisions — resolved

- **RBRV_VESSEL_MODE + RBRV_EGRESS_MODE** → `rbnve_*`. In.
- **RBRN_ENTRY_MODE / UPLINK_DNS_MODE / UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE** → `rbnne_*`.
  In. Their values are pure comparison tokens with no second life, so the
  prefix is free (no indirection needed).
- **BURN_PLATFORM** → harmonize `bubep_*` to `bunne_*` in the same sweep.
  Decided harmonize over grandfather, per load-bearing-complexity
  discipline — eliminates the two-shape wart rather than freezing it.
- **RBRN_RUNTIME (`docker`/`podman`) — EXCLUDED, stays bare.** Its value
  is consumed as the literal executable name (`command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`,
  `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect`). Adopting the convention would require a
  permanent value→binary indirection layer at every dispatch site — a
  runtime cost paid for grep-discoverability of two values that
  `RBRN_RUNTIME` itself already marks. The convention would cost more than
  it returns. This is the one regime enum that does not earn the prefix;
  the exclusion is deliberate, not an oversight.
- **BURE_VERBOSE / BURE_COLOR** — out (bare ints, different problem class;
  unchanged from original framing).

## Comment / use-site classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on a value yields four classes. The migration paces walk
the candidate list and classify each hit:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the airgap
   build" — refer to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave alone.**
2. **Enum-value references** — equality tests, case arms, JSON literals,
   `vessel mode is conjure`, defaults — the actual variable value.
   **Migrate.**
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`,
   vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`, test-step labels like
   `ordain-conjure` — the bare word is part of an identifier. **Leave
   alone.**
4. **Coupled derived strings** — the value drives a derived token that
   lives in resource-name space and must stay bare even though it is
   semantically the enum value. **Leave the derived string bare; migrate
   only the dispatch that selects it.** Two confirmed witnesses:
   - egress → worker-pool suffixes (`RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_*`, the `-tether`/
     `-airgap` fragments in pool paths)
   - vessel-mode → hallmark prefixes (`RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_*` = `c`/`b`/`g`)
   Because of class 4, even a class-2 case arm is not blind find/replace:
   the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays
   `-tether`.

### Three judgment traps a naive grep misses

- **Default-value defaults** — `${z_mode:-conjure}` is class-2 but a
  `== "conjure"` grep skips it. The recipe must also grep `:-conjure`,
  `:-bind`, etc.
- **AsciiDoc wire-value tables read like prose** — a spec table cell
  documenting `(conjure, bind, or graft)` as a substitution value is
  class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave
  stale.
- **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — the onboarding-scenario fixtures
  are full of step labels (`ordain-conjure`, `wrest-bind`) and sigil
  consts that stay bare. Only the `export RBRV_*_MODE="..."` baseline
  strings in the fast-fixture file actually migrate; that pace rebuilds
  the Rust crate (`tt/vow-b`).

### Noise ranking (classification effort)

Counterintuitively `conjure` is the noisiest value (heavy phase/operation
prose), then `bind` (English-word noise: bind-mount, IP bind), then
`graft`; `tether`/`airgap` are the cleanest (~10 sites each, mostly
class-2 or class-4). This is why egress is the cheap pilot and vessel-mode
is the heavy classification grind.

## Cloud Build detection gap — vessel-mode acceptance gate

Vessel-mode's value crosses a substitution boundary
(`_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE` / `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`) into a **remote execution
context** — Cloud Build step shells and Python steps compare against the
literal value. The local `regime-validation` net catches the
declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep, but it does **not** exercise the
remote-side comparisons. A missed step-shell or Python site fails silently
until an actual conjure/graft cloud build runs.

**Consequence:** for the vessel-mode pace, "gauntlet green" is not
sufficient proof of done. Its acceptance gate is an actual **conjure +
graft cloud build** (the full ordain path), not just the fast/crucible
suites.

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — authoritative
  declarations; cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations to confirm no
  enum missed.
- Per value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/ tt/` filtered by extension for
  code sites; also `grep -rn ':-V\b'` for default-value defaults.
- Comment/prose: `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b'` (bash/Python), `'//.*\bV\b'`
  (Rust), and scan `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` for prose + wire-value
  tables.
- Substitution boundary: trace `_RBG[AV]_VESSEL_MODE` /
  `_RBG[AV]_EGRESS*` through `rbfd_*`/`rbfv_*` step assembly into the
  `rbgja*`/`rbgjv*` Python+shell steps.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform on
execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place. Operator
pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, and
`gauntlet` catch any local bare-value site, because the enum-validation
gate rejects a non-prefixed value once the declaration is updated. The
remote Cloud Build comparisons are the exception — see the detection gap
above.

## Heat-shape note — ₣BK relationship (BK coding landed)

₣BK (moorings-cutover) swept essentially this heat's file surface
(`Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `rbtd/src/*.rs`, `RBS*.adoc`) — but for path literals,
not enum values. The two heats are **semantically orthogonal** (different
concerns, mostly different lines). ₣BK's *coding* has landed (BKAAI
wrapped); the prior file-surface-churn concern is now historical — the tree
is settled and the re-validation confirmed the enum-value sites survived
intact.

What remains and matters:
- **Start gate:** ₣BK's two-platform gauntlet acceptance is still pending.
  Begin this heat only after ₣BK's gauntlet passes on linux + macos and any
  cutover bugfixes land — so a known-good cutover baseline exists and every
  ₣BM gauntlet signal is unambiguously about enums, not the cutover.
- **Residual churn risk:** only late ₣BK bugfixes from its gauntlet runs.
  Low; re-grep before editing if BK commits land meanwhile.
- The bubep→bunne harmonize touches BUK files (₣BK's other subtree). ₣BK's
  BUK code has landed, so no hard coordination remains — just awareness of
  any late bugfix churn there.

## What done looks like

- All in-scope bare enum values replaced with sprue-prefixed values across
  bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build), and Rust; `RBRN_RUNTIME` left bare.
- `buv_enum_enroll` (and gated `buv_gate_enroll`) declarations updated to
  expect the new values; regime files (`rbrv.env`, `rbrn.env`, BURN
  profiles) updated in place.
- Class-4 derived strings (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes) left bare;
  their selecting dispatch migrated.
- `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` green.
- Vessel-mode additionally proven by a real conjure + graft cloud build.
- A single grep per family returns the complete cluster:
  `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`, `grep -rn rbnne_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/` — and `grep -rn bubep_` returns nothing.

## Out of scope

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes, JSON
  wire-format keys, etc.) — separate heat.
- `RBRN_RUNTIME` (`docker`/`podman`) — executable-name coupling, excluded
  by decision above.
- `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` integer values — different problem class.
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind.

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Resolved cadence is per-variable-family, ordered cheap-pilot-first:

1. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec section,
   citing `mcm_sprue` + `mcm_inlay` for grounding, plus the class-4
   "coupled derived string stays bare" rule (two witnesses) and the
   `RBRN_RUNTIME` exclusion rationale.
2. Egress-mode pilot — `rbnve_tether`/`_airgap`. Smallest, cleanest;
   proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction.
3. Vessel-mode — `rbnve_bind`/`_conjure`/`_graft`. Heaviest; crosses
   bash/adoc/Python(Cloud Build)/Rust. Acceptance gate = real conjure +
   graft cloud build.
4. Node-mode — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS. High English-word noise,
   heavy classification.
5. bubep → bunne harmonize — BUK files; last slice.
6. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit.

Slate as thin dockets at mount-time (depth in slate commit messages); do
not pre-cut detailed site lists from this paddock.

content.text:
## ₣BM mounted — final pace ₢BMAAF (final-regression-and-grep-audit)

**Pace goal:** Prove the entire sprue migration end-to-end through the gauntlet, then verify the discoverability promise — one grep per family returns the complete cluster, and zero bare enum values survive.

**Heat context** — `rbk-12-mvp-regime-sprues`, 5/6 done. The migration itself has all landed: the convention is codified in BUS0 (₢BMAAA), vessel-mode → `rbnve_*` (₢BMAAC), node-mode → `rbnne_*` (₢BMAAD), and the `bubep_` → `bunne_` harmonize (₢BMAAE, plus a missed-site cleanup at ₢BMAAF's prior commit). This pace is the **gauntlet exit** — a failure here is a real migration bug (a missed site), not exploration.

**Name assessment:** `final-regression-and-grep-audit` fits the docket exactly. No rename needed.

### Docket (full)

The recipe is two parts:

1. **Run the gauntlet** — `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`, with pre-flight `rbw-MZ` and all crucibles quenched (burn-bridges posture).
2. **Grep audit** —
   - `grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/` / `rbnne_` / `bunne_` each return a complete, coherent cluster
   - `grep -rn bubep_ Tools/` returns nothing
   - Spot bare-value audit: surviving `conjure`/`graft`/`tether`/`airgap`/`disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` hits are all class-1/3/4 (prose, identifiers, coupled derived strings) — **zero class-2**.

**Acceptance:** gauntlet green + grep audit clean.

### Proposed approach
- **Grep audit first** (cheap, non-destructive) — confirms the migration's surface is clean before spending the gauntlet's wall-clock. Catches any straggler early.
- **Then the gauntlet** — but `rbw-MZ` (MarshalZeroes) zeroes the local regime to blank template, and the posture requires all crucibles quenched. That's a destructive pre-flight against your local state, so I'll want your go before running it.
- Triage any failure at opus grade — a red signal is a real missed site.

**Execution strategy:** Sequential opus — single-operator, the gauntlet is one long serial run and any triage is architectural.

Two things before I proceed:

questions.question: How do you want to handle the destructive gauntlet pre-flight (rbw-MZ zeroes the regime; all crucibles must be quenched)?

options.label: I'll run pre-flight myself
options.description: You run rbw-MZ and quench crucibles; I confirm state, then run the gauntlet and audit.

options.label: Do the grep audit only
options.description: I run the read-only grep audit now; we defer the gauntlet/pre-flight to when you're ready.

options.label: Run everything incl. rbw-MZ
options.description: I run rbw-MZ, quench crucibles, then the gauntlet. Destructive to local regime state.

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me compile the full report with all findings:

## Complete Gauntlet Test Infrastructure Map

### 1. ROUTING: rbw-tP → Orchestrator → Fixture

**Colophon Registration (rbw-tP QualifyPristine)**
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh:202`
- **Entry**: `buz_enroll RBZ_QUALIFY_PRISTINE "rbw-tP" "${z_mod}" "rbq_qualify_pristine" "" "Pristine qualify: gauntlet test suite (release gate)"`
- **Module**: `rbq_cli.sh` 

**Orchestrator (RBQ = Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh)**
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh:189-201`
- **Command**: `rbq_qualify_pristine()` 
- **What it does**: Invokes the gauntlet test suite via `"${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"` (line 198)

**Gauntlet Suite Definition (Shell Harness)**
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh:57-70`
- **Suite composition**:
  ```bash
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"      # Case 0: state-indifferent entry check
    "pristine-lifecycle"         # Cases 1-5: marshal-zero gate → depot → SA cycle → live-disqualify → tear-down
    "canonical-establish"        # Cases 2-5: canonical depot levy + SA suite
    "onboarding-sequence"        # Cases: handbook-walked vessel construction
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  ```

---

### 2. PRISTINE-LIFECYCLE FIXTURE DEFINITION & DEPOT CREATION SITES

**Fixture Definition**
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1329-1335`
- **Fixture name**: `pristine-lifecycle`
- **Disposition**: `StateProgressing` (fail-fast: any case failure halts all subsequent cases)
- **Setup/Teardown**: None

**Test Cases (5 cases)**
1. **Case 1: marshal-zero-attestation** (lines 645-687)
   - Validates tree clean, RBRR/RBRD blank, RBRA absent, nameplate hallmarks empty, vessel depot fields empty
   - Entry-contract gate for the gauntlet

2. **Case 2: depot-stand-up** (lines 728-846)
   - **DEPOT CREATION SITE #1**: Line 770-786
   - Installs throwaway prefixes, picks moniker, **levies GCP depot project**, re-lists, cross-checks project_id
   - Invokes: `rbw-dL` (RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_LEVY)

3. **Case 3: SA-cycle** (lines 856-1122)
   - Mantle governor, invest retriever & director, access-probes, divest both roles
   - Uses depot from case 2

4. **Case 4: depot-live-disqualify** (lines 1140-1205)
   - Tests BBAA9 contract: live refusal when target == RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID
   - Requires case 2 state (probe at line 1141-1148)

5. **Case 5: depot-tear-down** (lines 1216-1317)
   - **DEPOT CREATION SITE #2**: Line 1247-1263
   - Reads moniker from rbrd.env, rotates to placeholder, **invokes rbw-dU (unmake)** to delete the GCP project
   - Verifies depot absent or DELETE_REQUESTED state post-unmake

**Exact Depot Creation Lines**
- **Case 2 (stand-up)**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrp_pristine.rs:770-786`
  ```rust
  let levy = match rbtdrp_invoke_logged(
      ctx,
      RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_LEVY,  // rbw-dL
      &[],
      &[],
      dir,
      "levy",
  ) { ... }
  ```
  
- **Case 5 (tear-down)**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1247-1263`
  ```rust
  let unmake = match rbtdrp_invoke_logged(
      ctx,
      RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_UNMAKE,  // rbw-dU
      &[&project_id],
      &[(RBTDRI_BURE_CONFIRM_KEY, RBTDRI_BURE_CONFIRM_SKIP)],
      dir,
      "unmake",
  ) { ... }
  ```

---

### 3. CASE ENUMERATION & CASE EXCLUSION CAPABILITY

**Case Registry Structure** (Rust, rbtdre_Case is a function pointer)
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1321-1327`
- Cases are stored in a static array: `RBTDRP_CASES_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE`
- Each case is a function `fn(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict`
- Cases are enumerated by index in the case registry and executed sequentially

**Case Selection Framework** (Bash harness → Rust engine)
- **File**: `/Users/rbk/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/main.rs:163-275`
- Suite runner (`rbtdb_run_suite`): Runs all cases in a fixture
- Single-case runner (`rbtdb_run_single`): Can run one case by name
- **Case exclusion via:** `rbtd single <manifest> <fixture>` mode allows selecting individual cases
- **Fixture dispatch** (`rbtdrc_lookup_fixture`, line 129): Loads fixture from registry, case array is fixed

**Current Capability Assessment**
- **Per-case opt-out in single-case mode**: YES (tabtargets like `rbtd-r.FixtureRun.pristine-lifecycle.sh` with case name)
- **Fixture-level case exclusion (e.g., "run all EXCEPT case 2")**: NO direct support; would require:
  - Modifying `RBTDRP_CASES_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE` static array 
  - Creating a conditional case list at compile time, OR
  - Adding case-filter logic to the Rust engine dispatcher

**Pristine-lifecycle in suites**: 
- **Not in fast/service/crucible/complete suites** (lines 42-51 of rbte_engine.sh)
- **Only in gauntlet suite** (line 59)
- Gauntlet is orchestrated as a sequence by `rbte_suite()` (line 138), which is run-loop-invoked per fixture with `set -e` fail-fast

---

### 4. CANONICAL-ESTABLISH & RELEASE-QUALIFY CONTRAST

**Canonical-establish Fixture** (Second gauntlet fixture, §2 of release qualification)
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`
- **Cases**: 4 cases with precondition probes
  1. **Case 1: depot-levy** (line 442-550)
     - **ALSO CREATES GCP DEPOT**: Line 492-508
     - Installs canonical prefixes, levies a fresh canest depot (different family stem than pristine's `pristl`)
  2. Case 2: governor-mantle
  3. Case 3: retriever-invest
  4. Case 4: director-invest
- **Disposition**: StateProgressing (shares depot across all 4 cases)

**Release-Qualify Path (rbw-tr)**
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh:168-187`
- **Command**: `rbq_qualify_release()`
- **Phases**:
  1. Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  2. Shellcheck (expensive)
  3. Complete test suite (line 184): `"${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"`
- **Complete suite** includes: enrollment-validation, access-probe, hallmark-lifecycle, batch-vouch, tadmor, srjcl, pluml
- **Notably EXCLUDES**: pristine-lifecycle, canonical-establish, onboarding-sequence
- **Depot creation**: NO cloud projects created (complete suite doesn't include gauntlet fixtures)

---

### Summary Table

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| **rbw-tP dispatch** | rbz_zipper.sh:202 → rbq_cli.sh → rbq_Qualify.sh:189 |
| **Gauntlet composition** | 12 fixtures defined in rbte_engine.sh:57-70 |
| **Pristine lifecycle cases** | 5 sequential cases (lines 1321-1327 of rbtdrp_pristine.rs) |
| **Depot creation #1** | rbtdrp_pristine.rs:770-786 (case 2: stand-up, rbw-dL) |
| **Depot creation #2** | rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1247-1263 (case 5: tear-down, rbw-dU) |
| **Canonical also creates depot** | rbtdrk_canonical.rs:492-508 (case 1: levy, rbw-dL) |
| **Case exclusion support** | Single-case mode only; no suite-level filter (would need Rust engine changes) |
| **rbw-tr (Release) vs rbw-tP (Pristine)** | Release uses "complete" suite (NO gauntlet/depots); Pristine uses "gauntlet" suite (creates 2+ depots) |

- **Per-case opt-out in single-case mode**: YES (tabtargets like `rbtd-r.FixtureRun.pristine-lifecycle.sh` with case name)
- **Fixture-level case exclusion (e.g., "run all EXCEPT case 2")**: NO direct support; would require:
  - Modifying `RBTDRP_CASES_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE` static array 
  - Creating a conditional case list at compile time, OR
  - Adding case-filter logic to the Rust engine dispatcher

**Pristine-lifecycle in suites**: 
- **Not in fast/service/crucible/complete suites** (lines 42-51 of rbte_engine.sh)
- **Only in gauntlet suite** (line 59)
- Gauntlet is orchestrated as a sequence by `rbte_suite()` (line 138), which is run-loop-invoked per fixture with `set -e` fail-fast

---

### 4. CANONICAL-ESTABLISH & RELEASE-QUALIFY CONTRAST

**Canonical-establish Fixture** (Second gauntlet fixture, §2 of release qualification)
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`
- **Cases**: 4 cases with precondition probes
  1. **Case 1: depot-levy** (line 442-550)
     - **ALSO CREATES GCP DEPOT**: Line 492-508
     - Installs canonical prefixes, levies a fresh canest depot (different family stem than pristine's `pristl`)
  2. Case 2: governor-mantle
  3. Case 3: retriever-invest
  4. Case 4: director-invest
- **Disposition**: StateProgressing (shares depot across all 4 cases)

**Release-Qualify Path (rbw-tr)**
- **File**: `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh:168-187`
- **Command**: `rbq_qualify_release()`
- **Phases**:
  1. Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  2. Shellcheck (expensive)
  3. Complete test suite (line 184): `"${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"`
- **Complete suite** includes: enrollment-validation, access-probe, hallmark-lifecycle, batch-vouch, tadmor, srjcl, pluml
- **Notably EXCLUDES**: pristine-lifecycle, canonical-establish, onboarding-sequence
- **Depot creation**: NO cloud projects created (complete suite doesn't include gauntlet fixtures)

---

### Summary Table

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| **rbw-tP dispatch** | rbz_zipper.sh:202 → rbq_cli.sh → rbq_Qualify.sh:189 |
| **Gauntlet composition** | 12 fixtures defined in rbte_engine.sh:57-70 |
| **Pristine lifecycle cases** | 5 sequential cases (lines 1321-1327 of rbtdrp_pristine.rs) |
| **Depot creation #1** | rbtdrp_pristine.rs:770-786 (case 2: stand-up, rbw-dL) |
| **Depot creation #2** | rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1247-1263 (case 5: tear-down, rbw-dU) |
| **Canonical also creates depot** | rbtdrk_canonical.rs:492-508 (case 1: levy, rbw-dL) |
| **Case exclusion support** | Single-case mode only; no suite-level filter (would need Rust engine changes) |
| **rbw-tr (Release) vs rbw-tP (Pristine)** | Release uses "complete" suite (NO gauntlet/depots); Pristine uses "gauntlet" suite (creates 2+ depots) |

# Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"

lastPrompt: You remind me of a critical point: current gauntlet is not safe to run because we are extremely project lifcycle limited, and the gauntlet insists on creating 2 cloud projects per run.  I wonder that…

content.text:
I have the full picture now, and there's an important correction to your framing.

## The 2 cloud projects come from *two different fixtures*, not two steps in one

The gauntlet suite (`rbte_engine.sh:57-70`) runs 12 fixtures. **Two** of them levy a GCP depot project:

| Fixture | Creation site | Teardown? |
|---------|--------------|-----------|
| `pristine-lifecycle` | case 2 *depot-stand-up* (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs:770`, `rbw-dL`) | yes — case 5 *depot-tear-down* (`rbw-dU`) |
| `canonical-establish` | case 1 *depot-levy* (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs:492`, `rbw-dL`) | no teardown case |

So "remove the depot creation step from the pristine fixture" would eliminate only **one** of the two projects — `canonical-establish` would still levy the other. And it's not a clean excision: within `pristine-lifecycle`, cases 3/4/5 (SA-cycle, live-disqualify, tear-down) *all depend on* the depot stood up in case 2. Pull the creation and the rest of the fixture's arc collapses. Same coupling in `canonical-establish` — its 4 cases share the one levied depot.

So the right mechanism isn't "remove a step" — it's **omit the two depot-levying fixtures wholesale** via a new suite. The framework already supports this cleanly: add a `ZRBTE_SUITE_<name>` array (line 57) + a resolver arm (`rbte_engine.sh:100`) + a new tabtarget. No Rust changes, no broken case dependencies.

## But there's a scope question worth pausing on

Two concerns are tangled here:

**A — what ₢BMAAF actually needs.** This pace proves the sprue migration didn't miss a site. The mechanism that catches a missed site is the **enum-validation gate** — and that lives in `regime-validation`, `enrollment-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`, which are all in the **`fast` suite** and create **zero** cloud resources. The vessel-mode *cloud-build* acceptance gate was already a separate pace (₢BMAAC, landed). The paddock wrote "gauntlet green" as acceptance back when the gauntlet was assumed cheap. For *this* pace, `fast` + the grep audit arguably gives the real coverage cloud-free.

**B — the gauntlet footgun itself.** Defanging `rbw-tP` and building a no-cloud gauntlet variant is genuinely worth doing — but it's test-infrastructure surgery that reads like ₣BJ (rbk-14-mvp-testing-simplification) or ₣BB (rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification) work, not regime-sprues (₣BM).

So before I touch anything:

questions.question: How do you want to split the immediate ₢BMAAF verification from the gauntlet-defanging infrastructure work?

options.label: Fast+grep for ₢BMAAF, defang separately
options.description: Run `fast` suite (no cloud) + grep audit to satisfy ₢BMAAF now. Spin gauntlet-disable + no-cloud-suite into its own pace under ₣BJ or ₣BB. Keeps heats clean.

options.label: Do the infra surgery under ₣BM now
options.description: Disable rbw-tP (buc_die), add a no-cloud gauntlet suite + tabtarget, then run it for ₢BMAAF. Heavier; mixes test-infra change into the sprues heat.

options.label: Just disable the footgun now
options.description: Make rbw-tP buc_die immediately (cheap safety fix), run fast+grep for ₢BMAAF, defer the no-cloud-suite design to a later pace.

questions.question: For the no-cloud gauntlet variant, what should it omit?

options.label: Only the 2 depot-levying fixtures
options.description: Drop pristine-lifecycle + canonical-establish (the project-ID consumers). Keep onboarding-sequence/moriah/crucible fixtures, which consume cloud builds/GAR but no new projects.

options.label: All cloud-touching fixtures
options.description: Drop the 2 depot levies AND build/GAR-consuming fixtures (onboarding-sequence, moriah, hallmark-lifecycle) — leaves a purely-local validation+crucible suite. Cheapest, but less coverage.

options.label: Decide later
options.description: Not building the variant on this pass; skip this question.

content.text:
That read clarifies the coupling, and it raises a couple of genuine forks where your intent matters. Here's the dependency reality:

The pristine fixture's case 2 (`depot-stand-up`) isn't *just* the `rbw-dL` levy — it also installs throwaway prefixes, picks the next `pristl`-family moniker, and writes that moniker into `rbrd.env`. Cases 3 (SA-cycle) and 4 (live-disqualify) then read that moniker. So "the levy step" is entangled with setup the later cases depend on.

Also: case 1 (`marshal-zero-attestation`) *asserts blank state* — RBRD blank, depot fields empty. If you've manually levied a depot, case 1 will fail by design. And case 5 (`depot-tear-down`) runs `rbw-dU`, which deletes the project — defeating the point of a manually-reused depot.

So before I build `tT`:

questions.question: When you 'run the levy step manually,' do you mean just the rbw-dL call, or the whole stand-up setup (throwaway prefixes + pristl moniker written to rbrd.env, which cases 3/4 read)?

options.label: tT does setup, I do only rbw-dL
options.description: tT installs throwaway prefixes + picks/writes the moniker to rbrd.env, then stops and tells you to run rbw-dL by hand; later cases continue against it. You only ever run the bare project-creating call.

options.label: I run the full stand-up manually
options.description: You run an existing/standalone path that does prefixes+moniker+levy together; tT assumes a fully stood-up depot already exists and just runs the depot-consuming cases.

questions.question: Which pristine cases should tT actually run?

options.label: Only 3 (SA-cycle) + 4 (live-disqualify)
options.description: Skip case 1 (marshal-zero gate, conflicts with a pre-existing depot), skip case 2 (levy), skip case 5 (tear-down). Runs the depot-consuming cases against your manual depot.

options.label: Add case 1 (marshal-zero) at front
options.description: Include the zero-state attestation. NOTE: this only works if you run tT *before* the manual levy, then levy, then... — awkward ordering. Likely fails if a depot already exists.

options.label: Keep case 5 tear-down too
options.description: Run 3+4 then unmake the depot at the end. Cleans up each run, but deletes your manually-levied project so you'd re-levy (re-spend a project) every run.

questions.question: How should tT be built, given it's temporary?

options.label: New Rust fixture variant + tabtarget
options.description: Register a `pristine-tT` fixture reusing the existing case fns minus levy/teardown; add rbtd-r.FixtureRun.pristine-tT.sh. Clean, needs tt/vow-b rebuild. Easy to delete later.

options.label: Shell tabtarget driving single-case runs
options.description: A tT tabtarget that shells out to the existing single-case runner for cases 3 then 4 in sequence. No Rust change, but looser and relies on state persisting between invocations.

message.content: I'm impressed at your study, you indeed have noted some critical aspects.  I indeed do want to additionally 'comment out' case 1 as well as case 2, and have us manually do the throwaway prefix installation.  We won't need to do the depot renaming aspect, as the rest of this sequence runs just fine given a valid preexisting depot.  Did I forget to answer any of your questions?

content.text:
Yes — two things are still open, and one of them is load-bearing.

**What I now have from you (Q1 answered):** The operator does the full stand-up manually — throwaway prefix installation *and* the `rbw-dL` levy — so a valid depot already sits in `rbrd.env` before `tT` runs. `tT` does none of case 2's setup. We skip the moniker-pick/"renaming" because the manual depot already carries its moniker. Comment out **case 1** (marshal-zero gate — would false-fail against a live depot) and **case 2** (stand-up/levy). Cases 3 (SA-cycle) and 4 (live-disqualify) run against the preexisting depot. Good — that all hangs together.

**Still open:**

1. **Case 5 (`depot-tear-down`) — this is the real fork.** It runs `rbw-dU`, which *deletes* the depot project. When you said "the rest of this sequence runs just fine," that reads as cases 3, 4, **and 5**. But if case 5 runs, every `tT` invocation deletes your manually-levied depot — so you'd re-levy (re-spend a project) every single run, which is exactly the churn we're trying to avoid. The conservation goal points the other way: also comment out case 5, run `tT` repeatedly against one depot, and you tear it down manually with `rbw-dU` when you're truly done. **Which do you want — keep case 5 (clean teardown each run, re-levy each run), or comment it out too (reuse one depot across many runs)?**

2. **Mechanism (my old Q3).** Your word "comment out" suggests literally commenting the case entries out of the shared registry array (`RBTDRP_CASES_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE`, `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:1321`) — which is the lightest temporary touch, but it also trims the `pristine-lifecycle` fixture *everywhere it's used* (i.e., the gauntlet's copy too). Since you're shelving the gauntlet anyway that's probably fine — but confirm you're OK with the shared edit, versus me adding a separate `pristine-tT` fixture/array that leaves the original intact. And `tT` itself — a `rbtd-r.FixtureRun.pristine-tT.sh` tabtarget (or do you want it under the `rbw-` colophon family)?

Once you settle case 5 and confirm the mechanism, I have everything.

message.content: Hold on, I think you just caught me in a serious confusion.  I said the 'pristine' fixture, but isn't there another fixture that doesn't do the unmake of the depot?

content.text: Good instinct — let me verify the canonical fixture's case list rather than rely on the earlier summary.

input.description: Show canonical fixture case registry and functions

content.text:
Confirmed — your memory is right. That fixture is **`canonical-establish`** (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).

Its case registry (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs:787`) is:

| # | Case | Cloud effect |
|---|------|--------------|
| 1 | `rbtdrk_depot_levy` | levies depot (`rbw-dL`) — **creates** project |
| 2 | `rbtdrk_governor_mantle` | governor SA |
| 3 | `rbtdrk_retriever_invest` | retriever SA |
| 4 | `rbtdrk_director_invest` | director SA |

`teardown: None`, and there is **no unmake case** — it levies a depot and *leaves it standing*. That's the structural difference from `pristine-lifecycle`, which bookends with `depot-tear-down` (`rbw-dU`).

So this meaningfully simplifies the accommodation, and your instinct to switch fixtures is the cleaner path:

- **Base `tT` on `canonical-establish`, not `pristine-lifecycle`.** Comment out only **case 1 (`depot-levy`)**. You manually install the canonical throwaway prefixes + levy once; `tT` runs cases 2–4 (governor/retriever/director invest) against that standing depot.
- **No marshal-zero gate to fight** — canonical has no case 1 zero-state attestation, so there's nothing that false-fails against a live depot.
- **No unmake to delete your depot** — canonical never tears down, so the depot survives across as many `tT` runs as you like. You `rbw-dU` by hand when truly done.

That dissolves both of the thorny forks from `pristine` (the marshal-zero conflict and the case-5 deletion). It does test *different* machinery — governor mantle + SA investment rather than SA-cycle + live-disqualify — so worth a beat:

content.text:
Close — but it's actually a *third* fixture. The container builds live in **`onboarding-sequence`** (`rbtdro_onboarding.rs`), which is neither pristine nor canonical. You're right that it's *not* pristine; let me lay out the real chain.

`RBTDRO_CASES_ONBOARDING_SEQUENCE` (`rbtdro_onboarding.rs:1234`) is where every crucible image gets built:

| # | Case | Build kind | Produces image for |
|---|------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | `inscribe_reliquary` | cloud (mirror to GAR) | — |
| 2 | `kludge_tadmor` | **local** (no GCP) | **tadmor** |
| 3 | `kludge_ccyolo` | **local** (no GCP) | ccyolo |
| 4 | `ordain_conjure_sentry` | cloud conjure | sentry for moriah/srjcl/pluml |
| 5 | `ordain_conjure_jupyter` | cloud conjure | **srjcl** |
| 6 | `ordain_airgap_chain` | cloud airgap | **moriah** |
| 7 | `ordain_bind_plantuml` | cloud bind | **pluml** |
| 8 | `ordain_graft_demo` | graft (push local→GAR) | — |

So the gauntlet's real dependency chain is three fixtures deep:

```
canonical-establish   → levy depot + invest governor/retriever/DIRECTOR SAs
        ↓ (director credential + standing depot)
onboarding-sequence   → kludge (local) + ordain conjure/airgap/bind (CLOUD builds into the depot)
        ↓ (built crucible images)
tadmor / moriah / srjcl / pluml  → charge those images + run cases
```

The crucial point for your conservation goal: onboarding-sequence's cloud ordains **consume the existing depot — they don't create a new project.** So one manually-levied depot feeds the whole chain. (They *do* spend cloud-build minutes + GAR, but zero project-ID churn.)

**So yes — I see exactly how.** `tT` can't be a single-fixture run; it has to be a **suite** (new `ZRBTE_SUITE_TT` array + resolver arm in `rbte_engine.sh:100` + a `TestSuite` tabtarget). Proposed composition:

```
canonical-establish   (case 1 depot-levy commented out → reuses your manual depot)
onboarding-sequence   (the container builds)
tadmor
moriah
srjcl
pluml
```

That's essentially the gauntlet **minus `pristine-lifecycle`** (the redundant levy-and-destroy fixture) and **minus the validation-only fixtures** (enrollment/regime/regime-smoke/dockerfile/hallmark), with canonical's levy disabled so nothing creates a project.

Two things to confirm before I build it:

1. **The validation-only fixtures** — do you want `enrollment-validation` / `regime-validation` / `regime-smoke` / `dockerfile-hygiene` folded into `tT` too (they're free — no cloud, and they're the enum-coverage net for the sprue migration)? Or keep `tT` strictly the depot→build→crucible chain and run those via the existing `fast` suite separately?
2. **`tadmor` alone needs no cloud** (local kludge). If on some runs you only want the local crucible path and not the cloud-built moriah/srjcl/pluml, do you want a leaner variant too — or is one `tT` covering the full chain enough?

content.text:
Checked thoroughly. **No existing fixture or suite both builds and runs tadmor.** Here's the gap precisely:

- **`tadmor` crucible fixture** (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2293`): `setup = charge_crucible` (charge only — confirmed earlier it doesn't kludge), runs the security cases, `teardown = quench`. **Assumes the image already exists.**
- **`onboarding-sequence`** kludges tadmor (case 2) but never runs the security cases.
- **`crucible` suite** (`fast + tadmor + srjcl + pluml`) runs the tadmor fixture but doesn't build — it inherits images from a prior build.

So the local tadmor flow today is two manual steps: `rbw-tK.KludgeCycle.tadmor.sh` (build) → `rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh` (charge + run + quench). Nothing bundles them.

And here's why tadmor is the *right* one to bundle: **it's the only crucible whose image is purely local kludge — zero cloud, zero depot, zero project.** moriah/srjcl/pluml all need cloud ordain (airgap/conjure/bind) for their images, so they can't go fully self-contained. A `kludge → charge → run security cases → quench` tabtarget for tadmor would be a genuinely cheap, fully-offline iteration loop — and yes, with `t` = tadmor it has the stronger claim to `tT`. 

So the day's plan is shaping into **two tabtargets**:

| Working name | What it is | Cloud cost | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| (gauntlet-lite) | Suite: `canonical(−levy)` + `onboarding-sequence` + 4 crucibles + 4 validation fixtures | Cloud builds, **no project creation** (reuses your manual depot) | new `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` array + resolver arm + `TestSuite` tabtarget; comment out canonical case 1 |
| `tT` (tadmor self-contained) | Build + run tadmor end-to-end | **none** — fully local | new fixture w/ `setup = kludge+charge` (reuse `rbtdro_kludge_nameplate` + `charge_crucible`), cases = tadmor security cases, `teardown = quench`; + `FixtureRun` tabtarget |

The gauntlet-lite suite then needs its own name (not `tT`) since `tT` migrates to the tadmor one.

Two quick decisions before I write anything:

1. **The tadmor self-contained mechanism** — I lean toward a new Rust fixture (`tadmor-selfcontained` or similar) reusing the existing kludge + charge fns, because it slots cleanly into the suite/fixture framework and the FixtureRun tabtarget pattern. The looser alternative is a shell tabtarget that just chains `rbw-tK` then `rbtd-r tadmor`. Which fits your taste for these — clean fixture, or quick chain?
2. **Are we planning (slating these as paces under ₣BM) or building today?** You said "plan today" — do you want me to slate these two as paces now, or keep designing first?

content.text:
Great instinct to ask — the finding actively reshapes the plan.

## Case-sharing is trivial, and the codebase *already does it*

`rbtdre_Fixture.cases` is `&'static [rbtdre_Case]` (`rbtdre_engine.rs:97`), and `rbtdre_Case` is just a struct wrapping a function pointer (the `case!` macro at `:103` builds one). A fixture merely *references* a case array — so two fixtures pointing at the same array, or composing new arrays from shared case fns, is free.

And it's not hypothetical. Right there at `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2293`–`2311`:

```rust
RBTDRC_FIXTURE_TADMOR { setup: charge, cases: RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY, ... }
RBTDRC_FIXTURE_MORIAH { setup: charge, cases: RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY, ... }  // same array
```

with the comment: *"runtime semantics are identical to tadmor so the fixture shares `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY`. The distinction is provenance (cloud-built airgap vs local kludge), not behavior."* So the exact pattern you're reaching for is a blessed, in-tree idiom.

## This kills the "comment out" hack — both artifacts become *durable*

Your hunch is right: with case-reuse, neither piece needs to be temporary, and neither needs to mutate a shared fixture.

- **tadmor self-contained**: new fixture = `cases: RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` (verbatim reuse) + a new `setup` fn that kludges *then* charges + shared `quench` teardown. One new fn, one struct. It's literally "tadmor but with a build in setup" — same provenance-vs-behavior split the moriah comment describes.

- **mini-gauntlet**: instead of commenting case 1 out of the shared `canonical-establish` (which would break the real gauntlet's levy), define a **`canonical`-no-levy fixture** whose `cases` array reuses the three invest fns (`governor_mantle`, `retriever_invest`, `director_invest`) and simply *omits* `depot_levy`. The real gauntlet keeps its levy untouched; the mini-gauntlet reuses a standing depot you levy once by hand.

So the mini-gauntlet is the full gauntlet **minus `pristine-lifecycle`** (the create-and-destroy fixture) and **minus canonical's levy**, run against a persistent depot — zero project churn, but otherwise the same depot→build→crucible coverage. That's a legitimately useful permanent suite, not a stopgap. Good call.

## Naming

Suite names in this tree are plain single words (`fast`/`service`/`crucible`/`complete`/`gauntlet`), so no prefix needed — just something that fits the martial-trial register of "gauntlet." Candidates:

- **skirmish** — a smaller engagement before the main battle; pairs naturally with "gauntlet." My lead pick.
- **spar** — a practice fight; emphasizes "rehearsal, not the real gate."
- **bout** — a single contest; short, punchy.
- **drill** — rehearsal/exercise; plainer, less martial-flavored.

(Avoided `foray` — JJK already owns it for remote dispatch.)

Want me to go with **skirmish** for the durable mini-gauntlet suite and keep **tT**/`tadmor`-self-contained as the local smoke fixture? Once you pick the name I'll start building — and I'll notch against ₣BM as we go, no pace.

=== rbq qualify_pristine + release pattern ===
    # End of exempt list
  rbq_qualify_colophons
  rbq_qualify_context
  rbrn_preflight

  buc_step "Fast qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_release() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running release qualification"

  # Phase 1: Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  rbq_qualify_fast

  # Phase 2: Shellcheck (expensive, release-only)
  buc_step "Running shellcheck"
  buq_shellcheck "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" \
    "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc" \
    "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt"

  # Phase 3: Complete test suite (sequential)
  buc_step "Running complete test suite"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"

  buc_step "Release qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_pristine() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # The gauntlet's first content fixture (pristine-lifecycle case 1) is the
  # entry-contract gate: tree clean, RBRR blank, RBRA absent, nameplate
  # hallmarks empty, vessel depot fields empty. Preceding enrollment-validation
  # is state-indifferent. The bash for-loop in rbte_suite() runs under set -e
  # so any fixture failure halts the gauntlet immediately.
  buc_step "Running pristine qualification (gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"

  buc_step "Pristine qualification passed"
}

file.content:
/// single-case tabtarget emits on missing/unknown fixture arg. Declaration
/// order is also the listing order operators see.
pub static RBTDRC_FIXTURES: &[&'static rbtdre_Fixture] = &[
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_TADMOR,
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_MORIAH,
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_SRJCL,
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_PLUML,
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE,
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_BATCH_VOUCH,
    &RBTDRC_FIXTURE_ACCESS_PROBE,
    &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION,
    &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_REGIME_VALIDATION,
    &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_REGIME_SMOKE,
    &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_DOCKERFILE_HYGIENE,
    &crate::rbtdrf_handbook::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_HANDBOOK_RENDER,
    &crate::rbtdrp_pristine::RBTDRP_FIXTURE_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE,
    &crate::rbtdrk_canonical::RBTDRK_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_ESTABLISH,
    &crate::rbtdro_onboarding::RBTDRO_FIXTURE_ONBOARDING_SEQUENCE,
    &crate::rbtdrl_calibrant::RBTDRL_FIXTURE_VERDICTS,
    &crate::rbtdrl_calibrant::RBTDRL_FIXTURE_FAIL_FAST,
    &crate::rbtdrl_calibrant::RBTDRL_FIXTURE_PROGRESSING,
    &crate::rbtdrl_calibrant::RBTDRL_FIXTURE_SENTINEL,
];

/// Resolve a fixture name to its registered Fixture definition. Returns None
/// for unregistered names; callers decide whether that is fatal.
pub fn rbtdrc_lookup_fixture(fixture: &str) -> Option<&'static rbtdre_Fixture> {
    RBTDRC_FIXTURES.iter().find(|f| f.name == fixture).copied()
}

content.text: The registry is clear (`RBTDRC_FIXTURES` at `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2358` references fixtures from every module). One mechanic remains that decides the tadmor-self-contained design: how the charge/bark machinery resolves *which nameplate* — fixture-name or a separate field. That determines whether a new fixture can target the `tadmor` nameplate.

lastPrompt: I love 'skirmish', yes that!  if we haven't used `tS` then that would be perfect.  Proceed if so, else lets chat some more; okay to ask more questions first if you have them too

content.text:
Both `tS` and `tT` are free — **skirmish** it is. The skirmish suite design is clean and ready to build (the `canonical`-no-levy fixture is exactly the moriah/tadmor case-reuse idiom — reuse the three invest case fns, omit `depot_levy`, no nameplate coupling since canonical cases act on the depot/SAs, not a crucible). I can start that now.

But the `tT` (tadmor self-contained) piece hit a genuine wrinkle I should put in front of you before building — it changes the recommendation I gave earlier.

## The crucible cases are coupled to `fixture name == nameplate`

`rbtdri_invoke` (`rbtdri_invocation.rs:255`) resolves the nameplate from `ctx.fixture()`, and `ctx.fixture` is set to the **fixture's `name`** (the struct has no separate nameplate field). The shared `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` cases all charge/bark/writ via `rbtdri_invoke` — i.e., against `ctx.fixture()`. That's *why* tadmor and moriah work as separate fixtures: each fixture's `name` ("tadmor"/"moriah") **is** its nameplate.

So a self-contained fixture that reuses `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` must be `name: "tadmor"` for charge/bark to hit the tadmor nameplate — which **collides** with the existing `RBTDRC_FIXTURE_TADMOR` in the registry (lookup is by name). The clean "new fixture reusing the cases" idea I endorsed earlier doesn't fit the crucible the way it fits canonical. Two clean ways out:

**Option A — orchestration command (my new recommendation).** `rbw-tT` routes to a theurge/rbq command that chains: kludge tadmor (`rbw-tK`) → run the *existing* `tadmor` fixture (charge + security cases + quench). Zero fixture-name collision, reuses everything as-is, ~no Rust-fixture code. The "build" is simply prepended at the command level. This is the same shape `rbq_qualify_pristine` already uses (a command that drives a suite tabtarget).

**Option B — generalize the engine.** Add an optional `nameplate` to `rbtdre_Fixture` (defaulting to `name`), have the engine set `ctx.fixture` from it. Then a real `tadmor-build` fixture with `nameplate: "tadmor"` can reuse `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY`. Cleaner *conceptually* and more durable, but it's a framework change touching the struct + engine + all ~18 existing fixture literals (Rust requires every literal to add the field). Heavier.

I lean **A** — it's the least code, fully reuses the existing tadmor fixture, and gives you the real `rbw-tT` colophon. B is the "right" generalization if you expect to want name≠nameplate fixtures repeatedly, but that's speculative today.

So: **shall I (1) start building skirmish now, and (2) do `tT` as Option A?** Or if Option B's durability appeals — or you'd rather I hold skirmish too — let's settle it here.

message.content: A is closer to what I want, I think.  B seems the opposite of generalization, by coupling in zero or one nameplates there rather than just having a list of first class function names.  Am I making sense?

content.text:
Yes, you're making complete sense — and it's a sharp catch.

Option B doesn't generalize anything; it just *relocates* the coupling. Today the nameplate is bound implicitly through `name` (→ `ctx.fixture()`); B moves that binding to an explicit `Option<nameplate>` field. Either way the cardinality is still **exactly 0-or-1 nameplate per fixture**, decided at definition time. Adding a field to pin one optional value isn't more general than deriving it from the name — it's the same constraint with extra ceremony.

The thing you're reaching for — "just a list of first-class function names" — is the genuinely general shape: cases are already first-class fn pointers, so the general move is to make **nameplate a runtime argument that flows to the cases**, letting any case-list run against any nameplate with no per-nameplate struct at all. The fixture struct stops being the unit of "what + against what" and you compose `(cases, nameplate)` at call time. That's real generalization; B is indirection cosplaying as it.

The catch is that *true* generalization is a real refactor: cases are `fn(&Path) -> Verdict` and receive the nameplate implicitly via `ctx.fixture()`, so you'd thread an explicit nameplate through the case signature or the runner. Not worth it today — and **A makes the whole question moot** by composing at the command level (kludge, then drive the existing `tadmor` fixture) instead of touching the fixture model at all. So A isn't just the cheaper path; it sidesteps a coupling that would need a deeper fix to do "right." Good instinct to reject B.

**So: A it is.** Here's the concrete build, with the names I need to mint — and since you're the naming authority here and these are expensive to change, one quick confirm before I write code:

**Skirmish suite (`rbw-tS`):**
- New fixture in `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`: case array reusing `governor_mantle` + `retriever_invest` + `director_invest` (omitting `depot_levy`), registered in `RBTDRC_FIXTURES`. **Proposed fixture name: `canonical-invest`** (the investiture cases, sans levy).
- `ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH` = `enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke, dockerfile-hygiene, canonical-invest, onboarding-sequence, tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml` + resolver arm.
- `rbw-tS` → `rbq_qualify_skirmish` (zipper enroll + rbq cmd) → drives `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh`.

**Tadmor self-contained (`rbw-tT`, Option A):**
- `rbw-tT` → **`rbq_qualify_tadmor`** → `rbw-tK KludgeCycle tadmor` then `rbtd-r FixtureRun tadmor`.

Two naming calls for you: **`canonical-invest`** as the new fixture name, and **`rbq_qualify_skirmish` / `rbq_qualify_tadmor`** as the command names — good, or do you want different? Once you bless these I'll build both and notch against ₣BM.

#!/bin/bash

#
# RBQ CLI - Command line interface for RBQ qualification operations

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbq_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrn_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbq_Qualify.sh"

  zbuz_kindle
  zrbz_kindle
  zrbcc_kindle
  zrbq_kindle
}

buc_execute rbq_ "Recipe Bottle Qualification" zrbq_furnish "$@"

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buq_qualify.sh"

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBQ_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBQ_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbq_*)

zrbq_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq already kindled"

  zbuz_sentinel
  zrbz_sentinel
  zrbcc_sentinel

  test -n "${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURC_TABTARGET_DIR not set"
  test -n "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR:-}"     || buc_die "BURC_TOOLS_DIR not set"

  readonly ZRBQ_TT_DIR="${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/.."
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_DIR="${RBCC_KIT_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_launchers_subdir}/launcher.rbw_workbench.sh"

  readonly ZRBQ_KINDLED=1
}

zrbq_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbq not kindled - call zrbq_kindle first"
}
