#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_fixture="${1:-}"
  local z_case="${2:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST}" ${z_fixture:+"${z_fixture}"} ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

# eof

+  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
+  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical

+  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
+  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
+  # but creates no GCP project per run.

rbq_qualify_skirmish() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # Skirmish — the mini-gauntlet. Runs the depot->build->crucible chain against
  # a depot the operator has already levied by hand: canonical-invest skips the
  # levy and pristine-lifecycle is dropped, so no GCP project is created per
  # run (cloud build/GAR are still spent). The release gate stays rbw-tP; this
  # is the project-lifecycle-frugal substitute for iterative runs.
  # PRECONDITION: install canonical prefixes + levy a depot (rbw-dL) by hand.
  buc_step "Running skirmish qualification (mini-gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh"

  buc_step "Skirmish qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_tadmor() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Kludge the
  # tadmor vessel locally, then drive the existing tadmor crucible fixture
  # (charge + security cases + quench). Build and run are composed here at the
  # command level rather than as one fixture, because the crucible cases
  # resolve their nameplate from the fixture name (a separate fixture reusing
  # the security cases would have to be named "tadmor" and collide).
  buc_step "Kludging tadmor vessel (local build)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-tK.KludgeCycle.tadmor.sh"

  buc_step "Running tadmor crucible fixture (charge + security + quench)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh"

  buc_step "Tadmor self-contained qualification passed"
}

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBQ Qualify - Qualification orchestrator for tabtarget and colophon health

set -euo pipefail

ZRBQ_SCRIPT_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buq_qualify.sh"

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBQ_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBQ_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbq_*)

zrbq_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq already kindled"

  zbuz_sentinel
  zrbz_sentinel
  zrbcc_sentinel

  test -n "${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURC_TABTARGET_DIR not set"
  test -n "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR:-}"     || buc_die "BURC_TOOLS_DIR not set"

  readonly ZRBQ_TT_DIR="${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/.."
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_DIR="${RBCC_KIT_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_launchers_subdir}/launcher.rbw_workbench.sh"

  readonly ZRBQ_KINDLED=1
}

zrbq_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbq not kindled - call zrbq_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbq_*)

rbq_qualify_colophons() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying RBW colophon registrations"

  local z_fail_files=()
  local z_fail_reasons=()
  local z_checked=0

  local z_file=""
  for z_file in "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}"/*.sh; do
    test -e "${z_file}" || continue

    local z_basename="${z_file##*/}"

    # Load file lines (load-then-iterate)
    local z_lines=()
    local z_line=""
    while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
      z_lines+=("${z_line}")
    done < "${z_file}"

    # Only check tabtargets using the RBW workbench launcher
    local z_is_rbw=0
    if (( ${#z_lines[@]} > 1 )); then
      case "${z_lines[1]}" in
        *"${ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER}"*) z_is_rbw=1 ;;
      esac
    fi
    test "${z_is_rbw}" = "1" || continue

    z_checked=$((z_checked + 1))

    # Extract colophon from filename (everything before first delimiter)
    local z_colophon="${z_basename%%.*}"

    # Check colophon exists in z_buz_colophon_roll registry
    local z_found=0
    local z_i=0
    for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
      test "${z_buz_colophon_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_colophon}" || continue
      z_found=1

      # Verify module file exists
      local z_module="${z_buz_module_roll[$z_i]}"
      test -f "${ZRBQ_RBW_DIR}/${z_module}" || {
        z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
        z_fail_reasons+=("module not found: ${z_module}")
      }
      break
    done

    test "${z_found}" = "1" || {
      z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
      z_fail_reasons+=("colophon '${z_colophon}' not registered in rbz_zipper")
    }
  done

  buc_log_args "Checked ${z_checked} RBW tabtargets"

  if (( ${#z_fail_files[@]} )); then
    local z_j=0
    for z_j in "${!z_fail_files[@]}"; do
      buc_warn "${z_fail_files[$z_j]}: ${z_fail_reasons[$z_j]}"
    done
    buc_die "Colophon qualification failed: ${#z_fail_files[@]} of ${z_checked} tabtargets"
  fi

  buc_log_args "All ${z_checked} RBW colophons registered"
}

rbq_qualify_context() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying generated context freshness"

  local -r z_committed="${ZRBQ_RBW_DIR}/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md"
  local -r z_fresh="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbq_context_check.md"

  buz_emit_context "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" > "${z_fresh}" || buc_die "Failed to generate context for comparison"

  if ! test -f "${z_committed}"; then
    buc_die "Generated context file missing: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh"
  fi

  if ! diff -q "${z_fresh}" "${z_committed}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_die "Generated context file is stale: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh"
  fi

  buc_log_args "Context file is fresh"
}

rbq_qualify_fast() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running fast qualification"

  buq_tabtargets "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}" \
    "butctt.*.sh" \
    "buw-SI.*.sh" \
    "z-launcher.sh" \
    # End of exempt list
  rbq_qualify_colophons
  rbq_qualify_context
  rbrn_preflight

  buc_step "Fast qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_release() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running release qualification"

  # Phase 1: Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  rbq_qualify_fast

  # Phase 2: Shellcheck (expensive, release-only)
  buc_step "Running shellcheck"
  buq_shellcheck "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" \
    "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc" \
    "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt"

  # Phase 3: Complete test suite (sequential)
  buc_step "Running complete test suite"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"

  buc_step "Release qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_pristine() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # The gauntlet's first content fixture (pristine-lifecycle case 1) is the
  # entry-contract gate: tree clean, RBRR blank, RBRA absent, nameplate
  # hallmarks empty, vessel depot fields empty. Preceding enrollment-validation
  # is state-indifferent. The bash for-loop in rbte_suite() runs under set -e
  # so any fixture failure halts the gauntlet immediately.
  buc_step "Running pristine qualification (gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"

  buc_step "Pristine qualification passed"
}

# eof

input.description: Check if canest/family-stem lives in regime files

content.text:
Notched. Now your question — and the answer is a clean "no, because the data flows the other way."

**`canest3` is a *producer* value, not a consumer of regime state.** `RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE` is the stem the canonical fixture uses to *mint* a fresh depot moniker (`rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker` → `rbtdrk_family_stem` → `rbtdrk_install_depot_moniker`), which it then **writes into** `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` in `rbrd.env`. So:

```
Rust const "canest3"  ──mints──▶  RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER = "canest3bhm100000"  ──▶  levy
```

`rbrd.env` holds the *generated output* (the specific moniker for the depot currently levied), not the family base. There's no `canest3` sitting in RBRR/RBRD for the Rust to read — reading it from there would invert the dependency. (RBRD's actual fields are just `RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX` + `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER`; no family-stem concept.)

**The duplication that actually bit us is Rust-internal**, and it's the test's fault, not a layering problem: `rbtdtk_canonical.rs:66` hardcodes the literal `"canest2"` as a "contract" assertion instead of asserting against `RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE`. Someone bumped the source const to `canest3` (the projectId-reservation collision) and didn't update the test literal. The one-line fix is to point the test at the const so it can't drift again — but that's the orthogonal stale test I flagged, your call (and possibly another officium's).

**Should `canest3` instead be regime-configurable** (so a bump needs no recompile)? I'd say no — it's test-harness-internal naming, not consumer config. Surfacing it into a regime file would leak theurge concerns onto the consumer's regime surface; the occasional recompile-on-bump is the cheaper cost.

Stopping here — skirmish + tT are built and committed (`99a76511c`). Runtime validation (`rbw-tf`, and an actual skirmish run against your depot) is queued for whenever you're ready.

lastPrompt: Ah... yes, magic string violation as per RCG.  So can we simply tie the string literal to the constant?

content.text:
**114/114 pass.** The magic string is gone — `rbtdtk_family_stem_value` now asserts the composition (`rbtdrk_family_stem("xyz") == format!("{}xyz", RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE)`) against the constant, so a future era-bump of the base can't desync it. The full theurge suite is green.

This was a one-file change (`rbtdtk_canonical.rs`, the test file — untouched by my earlier work, so the commit stays orthogonal). Want me to notch it against ₣BM as well?

message.content: yes.  Okay, time to try the tT now, give that a try.

content.text: Notching the test fix, then running `rbw-tT`.

input.params: {"identity": "BM", "files": ["Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdtk_canonical.rs"], "intent": "Fix RCG magic-string violation in rbtdtk_family_stem_value: the test pinned the literal 'canest2' (duplicating RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE), so the canest2->canest3 era-bump of the source const left the test falsely failing. Rewrote to assert the composition behavior (rbtdrk_family_stem('xyz') == format!('{}xyz', RBTDRK_FAMILY_STEM_BASE)) tied to the constant, dropping the tautological literal-equality assert. Theurge 114/114 green."}

content.text:
The charge log reveals the real cause — and it's mine, not the sentry. The `rbw` workbench runs a `qualify_fast` preflight gate before any command, and it aborted:

```
ERROR: Generated context file is stale: Tools/rbk/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md — run tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh
```

I added `rbw-tS`/`rbw-tT` to the zipper but didn't regenerate the tabtarget context that's derived from it. The gate caught exactly that. Let me regenerate, then retry.

content.text:
Found it — and it's a real design gap in my tT composition, not an environment issue. The charge now passes the freshness gate but hits a **second clean-tree assertion**:

```
rbob_charge ERROR: Nameplate has uncommitted changes: rbmm_moorings/tadmor/rbrn.env — commit before charging
```

The sequence inside `rbq_qualify_tadmor` is self-defeating:

1. **Kludge** asserts a clean tree (passes), builds, then **drives the new `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK` into `tadmor/rbrn.env`** → dirties it.
2. **Charge** asserts the nameplate's `rbrn.env` is committed → **fails**, because the kludge just dirtied it.

There's a mandatory **commit-the-hallmark step between build and charge**. The onboarding-sequence fixture handles exactly this — `rbtdro_kludge_nameplate` *commits* the driven hallmark before charging. My command chained `rbw-tK` → `rbtd-r` with no commit in between, so it can never charge its own fresh build. (The manual operator flow has the same step: kludge, commit hallmark, charge.)

The fix is to commit the driven hallmark between the two tabtargets in `rbq_qualify_tadmor`:

```bash
"${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-tK.KludgeCycle.tadmor.sh"
git -C "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}" add rbmm_moorings/tadmor/rbrn.env
git -C "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}" commit -m "rbw-tT: commit tadmor kludge hallmark before charge"
"${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh"
```

This matches how the system already treats hallmarks (committed artifacts), and it's a script-level commit like the onboarding fixture's. **Tradeoff: one hallmark commit per `rbw-tT` run** (timestamp-based, so each rebuild = a new commit — some log noise).

Before I add git plumbing to a qualify command — you've been particular about these calls, and there's the per-run commit-noise cost: **want me to go with that auto-commit, or would you rather tT leave the build uncommitted and just print "now commit rbrn.env and re-run" (operator commits)?** Also note the tree is currently dirty again from this run's kludge (`k260522064751-ab67ebadb`).

message.content: Does the fixture therefore need to do the notch?  I think there is precedent.

content.text: Yes — and that's the better answer. The precedent is exactly `rbtdro_kludge_nameplate` in the onboarding fixture, which I cited earlier ("Commit sentry hallmark before bottle kludge"). Let me confirm its shape, because if it both builds *and* commits, it dissolves two problems at once.

content.content:
547	/// Kludge helper: build sentry and bottle locally for a nameplate. Both steps
548	/// are local docker builds with no GCP dependency. The kludge tabtargets drive
549	/// hallmarks directly into the nameplate's rbrn.env via zrbob_drive_hallmark.
550	/// rbw-cKS and rbw-cKB are global (param1-channel) tabtargets — no imprint suffix,
551	/// nameplate passed as a positional argument.
552	fn rbtdro_kludge_nameplate(
553	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
554	    dir: &Path,
555	    nameplate: &str,
556	) -> Result<(), rbtdre_Verdict> {
557	    rbtdro_invoke_or_fail(
558	        ctx,
559	        &format!("{} {}", RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE, RBTDRM_CONTAINER_SENTRY),
560	        nameplate,
561	        RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
562	        &[nameplate],
563	        &[],
564	        dir,
565	        &format!("{}-{}-{}", RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE, RBTDRM_CONTAINER_SENTRY, nameplate),
566	    )?;
567	
568	    // Commit sentry hallmark before bottle kludge — kludge asserts clean tree.
569	    rbtdro_git_commit(&format!(
570	        "{}-{}: {} hallmark",
571	        RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE, nameplate, RBTDRM_CONTAINER_SENTRY
572	    ))?;
573	
574	    rbtdro_invoke_or_fail(
575	        ctx,
576	        &format!("{} {}", RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE, RBTDRM_CONTAINER_BOTTLE),
577	        nameplate,
578	        RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
579	        &[nameplate],
580	        &[],
581	        dir,
582	        &format!("{}-{}-{}", RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE, RBTDRM_CONTAINER_BOTTLE, nameplate),
583	    )?;
584	
585	    rbtdro_git_commit(&format!(
586	        "{}-{}: {} hallmark",
587	        RBTDRM_OPERATION_KLUDGE, nameplate, RBTDRM_CONTAINER_BOTTLE
588	    ))?;
589	    Ok(())
590	}
591

594	/// yokes the stamp into all ordain-side vessels in one pass and auto-commits.

lastPrompt: Does the fixture therefore need to do the notch?  I think there is precedent.

content.text:
Confirmed — `rbtdro_kludge_nameplate` is exactly the precedent: it kludges **sentry** (commit) **then bottle** (commit), via `rbtdro_git_commit`, taking the nameplate as an explicit param. So your instinct is right on two counts:

1. **The commit belongs in the fixture layer, not bash** — there's first-class precedent, and it keeps git plumbing out of the qualify orchestrator.
2. It also fixes a latent second bug: my `rbw-tK` chain only built the **bottle** — the sentry was never rebuilt. `rbtdro_kludge_nameplate` builds **both**, which is what charge actually needs.

This reshapes tT cleanly and dodges the name-collision: **tT becomes two fixtures** — a `kludge-tadmor` fixture (builds+commits both vessels, nameplate passed explicitly so no `ctx.fixture()` coupling) **then** the existing `tadmor` fixture (charge → clean tree ✓ → security → quench). The notch is the fixture's job, exactly as onboarding does it.

One thing to verify before I build it — whether the existing `rbtdro_onboarding_kludge_tadmor` case is reusable standalone or carries an onboarding-case-1 precondition probe:

Confirmed the caveat: that case carries a probe (`reliquary stamp captured`) that's an *onboarding-sequence* witness, not a local-kludge dependency — so I'll reuse the **impl** (`rbtdro_onboarding_kludge_tadmor_impl` → `rbtdro_kludge_nameplate`) via a thin probe-free case, not the guarded case itself.

So your read is right: **the fixture does the notch, with first-class precedent.** Building it now — a `kludge-tadmor` fixture (builds+commits both vessels), and `rbq_qualify_tadmor` runs it then the existing `tadmor` fixture. No bash git, no name collision, and the sentry actually gets built.

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBQ Qualify - Qualification orchestrator for tabtarget and colophon health

set -euo pipefail

ZRBQ_SCRIPT_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buq_qualify.sh"

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBQ_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBQ_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbq_*)

zrbq_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq already kindled"

  zbuz_sentinel
  zrbz_sentinel
  zrbcc_sentinel

  test -n "${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURC_TABTARGET_DIR not set"
  test -n "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR:-}"     || buc_die "BURC_TOOLS_DIR not set"

  readonly ZRBQ_TT_DIR="${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/.."
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_DIR="${RBCC_KIT_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_launchers_subdir}/launcher.rbw_workbench.sh"

  readonly ZRBQ_KINDLED=1
}

zrbq_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbq not kindled - call zrbq_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbq_*)

rbq_qualify_colophons() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying RBW colophon registrations"

  local z_fail_files=()
  local z_fail_reasons=()
  local z_checked=0

  local z_file=""
  for z_file in "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}"/*.sh; do
    test -e "${z_file}" || continue

    local z_basename="${z_file##*/}"

    # Load file lines (load-then-iterate)
    local z_lines=()
    local z_line=""
    while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
      z_lines+=("${z_line}")
    done < "${z_file}"

    # Only check tabtargets using the RBW workbench launcher
    local z_is_rbw=0
    if (( ${#z_lines[@]} > 1 )); then
      case "${z_lines[1]}" in
        *"${ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER}"*) z_is_rbw=1 ;;
      esac
    fi
    test "${z_is_rbw}" = "1" || continue

    z_checked=$((z_checked + 1))

    # Extract colophon from filename (everything before first delimiter)
    local z_colophon="${z_basename%%.*}"

    # Check colophon exists in z_buz_colophon_roll registry
    local z_found=0
    local z_i=0
    for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
      test "${z_buz_colophon_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_colophon}" || continue
      z_found=1

      # Verify module file exists
      local z_module="${z_buz_module_roll[$z_i]}"
      test -f "${ZRBQ_RBW_DIR}/${z_module}" || {
        z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
        z_fail_reasons+=("module not found: ${z_module}")
      }
      break
    done

    test "${z_found}" = "1" || {
      z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
      z_fail_reasons+=("colophon '${z_colophon}' not registered in rbz_zipper")
    }
  done

  buc_log_args "Checked ${z_checked} RBW tabtargets"

  if (( ${#z_fail_files[@]} )); then
    local z_j=0
    for z_j in "${!z_fail_files[@]}"; do
      buc_warn "${z_fail_files[$z_j]}: ${z_fail_reasons[$z_j]}"
    done
    buc_die "Colophon qualification failed: ${#z_fail_files[@]} of ${z_checked} tabtargets"
  fi

  buc_log_args "All ${z_checked} RBW colophons registered"
}

rbq_qualify_context() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying generated context freshness"

  local -r z_committed="${ZRBQ_RBW_DIR}/rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md"
  local -r z_fresh="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbq_context_check.md"

  buz_emit_context "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" > "${z_fresh}" || buc_die "Failed to generate context for comparison"

  if ! test -f "${z_committed}"; then
    buc_die "Generated context file missing: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh"
  fi

  if ! diff -q "${z_fresh}" "${z_committed}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_die "Generated context file is stale: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh"
  fi

  buc_log_args "Context file is fresh"
}

rbq_qualify_fast() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running fast qualification"

  buq_tabtargets "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}" \
    "butctt.*.sh" \
    "buw-SI.*.sh" \
    "z-launcher.sh" \
    # End of exempt list
  rbq_qualify_colophons
  rbq_qualify_context
  rbrn_preflight

  buc_step "Fast qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_release() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running release qualification"

  # Phase 1: Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight)
  rbq_qualify_fast

  # Phase 2: Shellcheck (expensive, release-only)
  buc_step "Running shellcheck"
  buq_shellcheck "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" \
    "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc" \
    "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt"

  # Phase 3: Complete test suite (sequential)
  buc_step "Running complete test suite"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"

  buc_step "Release qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_pristine() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # The gauntlet's first content fixture (pristine-lifecycle case 1) is the
  # entry-contract gate: tree clean, RBRR blank, RBRA absent, nameplate
  # hallmarks empty, vessel depot fields empty. Preceding enrollment-validation
  # is state-indifferent. The bash for-loop in rbte_suite() runs under set -e
  # so any fixture failure halts the gauntlet immediately.
  buc_step "Running pristine qualification (gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh"

  buc_step "Pristine qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_skirmish() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # Skirmish — the mini-gauntlet. Runs the depot->build->crucible chain against
  # a depot the operator has already levied by hand: canonical-invest skips the
  # levy and pristine-lifecycle is dropped, so no GCP project is created per
  # run (cloud build/GAR are still spent). The release gate stays rbw-tP; this
  # is the project-lifecycle-frugal substitute for iterative runs.
  # PRECONDITION: install canonical prefixes + levy a depot (rbw-dL) by hand.
  buc_step "Running skirmish qualification (mini-gauntlet test suite)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.skirmish.sh"

  buc_step "Skirmish qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_tadmor() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Kludge the
  # tadmor vessel locally, then drive the existing tadmor crucible fixture
  # (charge + security cases + quench). Build and run are composed here at the
  # command level rather than as one fixture, because the crucible cases
  # resolve their nameplate from the fixture name (a separate fixture reusing
  # the security cases would have to be named "tadmor" and collide).
  buc_step "Kludging tadmor vessel (local build)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-tK.KludgeCycle.tadmor.sh"

  buc_step "Running tadmor crucible fixture (charge + security + quench)"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh"

  buc_step "Tadmor self-contained qualification passed"
}

# eof

content.text: No rustfmt config and no `cargo fmt` anywhere in the build — that's *why* our manual one-per-line edits stuck (nothing reflows them). So a prose RCG discipline is the right vehicle. Let me read a couple of existing discipline sections to match RCG's voice before proposing wording.

content.content:
288	## Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings)
289	
290	String Boundary Discipline covers serialized strings. This section extends to **all** repeated values.
291	
292	### Rule
293	
294	Any magic value that appears 2+ times requires a const. This includes numbers, format strings, and computed values.
295	
296	### Derivation Rule
297	
298	If value B depends on value A, always derive B from A at the use site. Never define B as a separate constant.
299	
300	```rust
301	// ✅ Length derived from the const
302	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT: &str = "0000000";
303	let short_arg = format!("--short={}", JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len());
304	
305	fn zjjrg_is_commit_sha(s: &str) -> bool {
306	    s.len() == JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len() && s.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_hexdigit())
307	}
308	
309	// ❌ Magic number exists separately — drift risk
310	const COMMIT_LEN: usize = 7;  // Could diverge from JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT
311	format!("--short=7")          // Hardcoded, not derived
312	```
313	
314	### Identity Rule
315	
316	The const name must identify the specific value, not just its category. A category-only suffix is ambiguous and becomes a terminal exclusivity violation the moment a second value appears in the same category.
317	
318	```rust
319	// ✅ Suffix identifies the specific command
320	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME_GET_SPEC: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
321	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_BIND: &str = "jjx_bind";
322	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND: &str = "jjx_send";
323	
324	// ❌ Suffix names the role, not the identity — deferred terminal exclusivity debt
325	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
326	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_bind";  // Blocks JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND
327	```
328	
329	### Smell Test
330	
331	If `grep '7'` (or any literal) finds the same value used for the same purpose in multiple places, extract a const and derive.
332	
333	## Constructor Discipline
334	
335	When a struct requires non-trivial initialization (timestamps, captured values, computed defaults), define a constructor helper. All creation sites use the helper.
336	
337	### Rationale

lastPrompt: While we wait: twice in this conversation we adjusted rust code for better diffing.  I'm wondering how we could add requirements/guidelines in RCG to encourage this?

content.text:
RCG's pattern is clear: `## X Discipline` → `### Rule` (with ✅/❌ blocks) → `### Rationale` → `### Smell Test`. Here's a draft section in that voice:

---

## Import Discipline

`use` blocks churn more than almost anything else in a file — every new symbol referenced adds an import, every removed reference drops one. Format multi-item `use` braces **one item per line**, even when several would fit the line width.

### Rule

A braced `use` list with 2+ items places each item on its own line, trailing comma included:

```rust
// ✅ One per line — adding/removing a symbol is a single-line diff
use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
    RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR,
};

// ❌ Width-packed — adding one symbol reflows the line, and two branches each
//    adding an import to the same packed line collide on merge
use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
};
```

### Rationale

The diff of an import edit should be proportional to the change: one symbol added = one line added, never a reflow of neighbors. One-per-line keeps `git blame` precise (each import traces to the commit that introduced it) and removes the spurious merge conflicts width-packing creates.

### Note on rustfmt

This project does not run `cargo fmt`, so this discipline is review-enforced. Default rustfmt repacks these lists to fill the width; a project that later adopts `cargo fmt` must set `imports_layout = "Vertical"` (nightly) to preserve it.

### Smell Test

If a single `use` line carries more than one imported item, split it.

---

Three calls for you:

1. **Scope** — keep it `use`-only (both our edits were imports, and imports are the high-churn case), or generalize to "any comma-separated list expected to grow" (would also cover things like the `case!` arrays, though those are already one-per-line by our convention)? I lean **`use`-only** — narrowly load-bearing, doesn't invent a rule fighting rustfmt defaults across the board.
2. **Placement** — I'd put it right after **Crate Boilerplate** (both are top-of-file layout concerns), before File Naming. Alternative: group it with the style disciplines near Comment Discipline.
3. **The rustfmt note** — keep it (honest about the fragility), or drop it as noise since the project doesn't fmt?

Say the word and I'll fold it in. (Background `rbw-tT` still running — I'll surface its verdict the moment it lands.)

content.text:
Now I have the definitive answer — and it's a clean illustration of the project's own Load-Bearing Complexity principle.

## What rustfmt can actually do

The empirical results:

| Path | Result |
|------|--------|
| `--config imports_layout=Vertical` (CLI flag) | ✅ applies one-per-line |
| `rustfmt.toml` with `imports_layout = "Vertical"` on **stable** | ❌ **`Warning: can't set imports_layout = Vertical, unstable features are only available in nightly channel`** — silently ignored |

So:
- **Config goes in `rustfmt.toml`** at the project/crate root — **not** Cargo.toml. (Cargo.toml never holds rustfmt settings.)
- The option is `imports_layout = "Vertical"`, **but it's nightly-only.** Via `rustfmt.toml` (the path `cargo fmt` actually reads), **stable rustfmt ignores it.** Durable enforcement would require pinning a **nightly toolchain** and running `cargo +nightly fmt`.

## Why I'd still keep it prose, not rustfmt

The deeper problem isn't the nightly pin — it's that **rustfmt has no "imports only" mode.** Adopting `cargo fmt` to enforce this one rule means signing the *entire* codebase over to rustfmt's conventions, which would churn the project's deliberately hand-maintained style (the bespoke naming, the layout you've been curating two imports at a time). You'd trade one tidy import rule for a wholesale reformat you don't want.

By the project's own litmus — *does this element earn its existence?* — machine-enforcing one import-layout rule costs a nightly toolchain **plus** surrendering all formatting to rustfmt. That fails the test. The prose RCG discipline returns the benefit at none of the cost.

So my recommendation stands: **add the `Import Discipline` section as prose**, and I'll sharpen the rustfmt note to record exactly what we found (nightly-only, stable ignores it) so a future reader doesn't re-litigate this. Want me to fold it in with that correction — `use`-only scope, placed after Crate Boilerplate?

---

Also: **`rbw-tT` is fully working.** 59 security cases passed, 0 failed; the `kludge-tadmor` fixture built both vessels and committed their hallmarks itself (its own commits, like onboarding does), so the tree's clean. Both deliverables — `skirmish` and `tT` — are now built and the `tT` path is runtime-validated.

message.content: Don't do any more experiments.  from what you have already learned, do you suspect adding 'rustfmt' will cause a fight with existing RCG prose?  I want to start to determine if rustfmt is fully consistent with my own beliefs about what makes code maintainable.

content.text:
No more experiments — and I think the honest answer is yes, but the fight is narrower and more revealing than "rustfmt vs RCG" sounds. Here's how I'd frame it.

## rustfmt only governs one layer; most of RCG lives in others

rustfmt touches **whitespace and layout only** — line width, indentation, brace placement, blank lines, import packing/ordering, trailing commas. It does **not** touch identifiers, visibility, comment *content*, constant extraction, or file structure. So when you sort RCG's disciplines by layer:

- **Naming / minting** (the `non_camel_case_types` spine, prefix discipline) — rustfmt can't touch identifiers. The thing people *fear* ("rustfmt will 'fix' my snake_case types") is actually a rustc/clippy lint you've already `allow`-ed. **No conflict — different tool entirely.**
- **Semantics / structure** (Constant, String Boundary, Constructor, Visibility, Minting, File Size, Spec Authority) — rustfmt doesn't extract consts, derive values, or split files. **No conflict — orthogonal.**
- **Comments** — rustfmt won't reflow your comment prose or enforce RCG's prohibited/permitted rules. **Mostly orthogonal** (it may tweak comment indentation).
- **Layout** (the proposed Import Discipline; possibly Output's "Line Discipline") — **this thin layer is the entire overlap, and it's where the fight is.**

So ~90% of RCG is *immune* to rustfmt — not because they agree, but because they govern different layers. RCG would survive adoption untouched everywhere except the layout seam.

## The fight is real, localized, and the imports case is the proof

At that layout seam, rustfmt's defaults can directly oppose a documented belief:

- **Imports** — rustfmt packs to fill width; you want one-per-line for diff-locality. Proven conflict, and (the killer) **unfixable on stable** — the override is nightly-only.
- **`max_width`** — rustfmt rewraps long lines to its number; any deliberate long-line layout gets rewritten.
- **No vertical alignment** — rustfmt strips aligned columns; if you ever align things for readability, it'll undo it.
- **`reorder_imports`** (stable, default-on) — alphabetizes; if you ever group imports semantically, it overrides that.

## The sharper point for your determination

rustfmt isn't *one* philosophy you can accept or reject — it's an accreted bundle of independently-chosen community defaults, and **it isn't even self-consistent about your core belief.** Its trailing-comma default *serves* diff-locality (clean single-line additions); its import-packing default *opposes* it (one symbol reflows the line). Same tool, opposite stances on the same principle — because no single maintainability thesis drove them; they were each decided separately over years.

So "is rustfmt consistent with my beliefs?" can't resolve to yes/no. The real test is: **which of your maintainability beliefs are *layout-layer* beliefs?** Those are the only ones rustfmt can ever serve or violate — and for the ones it violates, stable rustfmt gives you no recourse. The imports belief is the first one you've surfaced, and it already loses on stable. That's a strong early signal that your guiding value (diff-locality / change-proportional edits) is *not* what rustfmt's layout defaults optimize for — they optimize for canonical compactness.

If you want to pursue this rigorously, the productive next move is to enumerate your layout-layer beliefs (diff-locality is one; what are the others — alignment? long-line tolerance? grouping?) and check each against whether it's a stable rustfmt knob. My prediction from what we've seen: most of the knobs you'd want are unstable, which means the question isn't "do I agree with rustfmt" but "am I willing to cede the layout layer to a tool I can't fully tune." Want to start listing those beliefs?

file.content:
## Crate Boilerplate

Every Rust file that serves as a crate root must include these attributes. This includes `lib.rs`, `main.rs`, and every integration test file in `tests/` (each compiles as a separate crate).

```rust
#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
#![allow(private_interfaces)]
#![deny(warnings)]
```

- `non_camel_case_types`: Allows prefixed type names like `jjrg_Gallops`
- `private_interfaces`: Allows z-prefixed (internal) types in public enum variants
- `deny(warnings)`: All warnings are build failures — no warning is acceptable in committed code

The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.

## File Naming

### Source Files

## File Naming

lines: The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.

+`use` blocks churn more than almost anything else in a file — every new symbol referenced adds an import, every removed reference drops one. Format them for **change-proportional diffs**: one item per line, alphabetically ordered.
+A braced `use` list with 2+ items places each item on its own line, in alphabetical order, with a trailing comma:
+// ✅ One per line, sorted — adding/removing a symbol is a single-line diff
+// ❌ Width-packed — adding one symbol reflows the line; two branches each
+//    adding an import to the same packed line collide on merge

+The diff of an import edit should be proportional to the change: one symbol added = one line added, never a reflow of neighbors. One-per-line keeps `git blame` precise (each import traces to the commit that introduced it) and removes the spurious merge conflicts width-packing creates. Alphabetical order makes the insertion point deterministic — there is no "where does this go" decision, and two branches adding different symbols sort to different positions, so they don't collide.
+This project does not run `cargo fmt`; this discipline is review-enforced. Two of rustfmt's behaviors bear on it:
+- **Alphabetization** is rustfmt's stable default (`reorder_imports`, on by default) — consistent with this rule.

+- **One-per-line** (`imports_layout = "Vertical"`) is **nightly-only**. On the stable toolchain, setting it in `rustfmt.toml` is rejected with a warning and silently ignored (measured: `Warning: can't set imports_layout = Vertical, unstable features are only available in nightly channel`). `max_width = 1` would force verticality but is a global sledgehammer — it maximally wraps *all* expressions, not just imports — so it is not a targeted substitute.

+Net: until a nightly `cargo fmt` is adopted (a larger decision — rustfmt has no imports-only mode and would reformat the whole codebase), one-per-line is held by review, alphabetization happens to match rustfmt's stable default.
+If a single `use` line carries more than one imported item, or the items are not in sorted order, split and sort.

On **`max_width = 1`**: it's a valid stable option value (it's just a `usize`), but it's the wrong tool — `max_width` is global, so 1 would force maximal wrapping of *every* construct (function calls, match arms, expressions), not just imports. So it "works" but as a sledgehammer, not a targeted import rule — which is why I recorded it in the RCG note as rejected rather than recommended.
